technology.description: Azure AKS Review is a GA family with 112 normalized items, 33 high-severity findings, covers Application Deployment, Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery, Cost Governance, and touches services like Azure Container Registry, Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS).
technology.whatThisMeans: Suitable for executive and architecture review packs by default.

quality.label: GA · High confidence
quality.summary: Strong review accelerator. Quality score 93 with 82% metadata completeness and 100% severity coverage.

items.text: Use canary or blue/green deployments
If required for AKS Windows workloads HostProcess containers can be used
Use KEDA if running event-driven workloads
Use Dapr to ease microservice development
Use automation through ARM/TF to create your Azure resources
Schedule and perform DR tests regularly
Use Azure Traffic Manager or Azure Front Door as a global load balancer for region failover
Use Availability Zones if they are supported in your Azure region
items.graph: resources | where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant= isnotnull(properties.agentPoolProfiles[0].availabilityZones) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Use the SLA-backed AKS offering
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (sku.tier=='Paid') | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Use Disruption Budgets in your pod and deployment definitions
If using a private registry, configure region replication to store images in multiple regions
Use Zone-Redundant Storage (ZRS) with stateful workloads
Define non-functional requirements such as SLAs, RTO (Recovery Time Objective) and RPO (Recovery Point Objective).
Use an external application such as kubecost to allocate costs to different users
Use scale down mode to delete/deallocate nodes
When required use multi-instance partitioning GPU on AKS Clusters
If running a Dev/Test cluster use NodePool Start/Stop
Use Azure Policy for Kubernetes to ensure cluster compliance
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (isnotnull(properties.addonProfiles.azurepolicy) and properties.addonProfiles.azurepolicy.enabled==true) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Separate applications from the control plane with user/system node pools
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | project id,resourceGroup,name,pools=properties.agentPoolProfiles | project id,name,resourceGroup,poolcount=array_length(pools) | extend compliant = (poolcount > 1)

items.text: Add taint to your system nodepool to make it dedicated
Use a private registry for your images, such as ACR
Scan your images for vulnerabilities
Use Azure Security Center to detect security posture vulnerabilities
If required configure FIPS
Define app separation requirements (namespace/nodepool/cluster)
Store your secrets in Azure Key Vault with the CSI Secrets Store driver
If using Service Principals for the cluster, refresh credentials periodically (like quarterly)
If required add Key Management Service etcd encryption
If required consider using Confidential Compute for AKS
Consider using Defender for Containers
Use managed identities instead of Service Principals
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (properties.servicePrincipalProfile.clientId=='msi') | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Integrate authentication with AAD (using the managed integration)
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = isnotnull(properties.aadProfile) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Limit access to admin kubeconfig (get-credentials --admin)
Integrate authorization with AAD RBAC
Use namespaces for restricting RBAC privilege in Kubernetes
For Pod Identity Access Management use Azure AD Workload Identity (preview)
For AKS non-interactive logins use kubelogin (preview)
Disable AKS local accounts
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (properties.disableLocalAccounts==true) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Configure if required Just-in-time cluster access
Configure if required AAD conditional access for AKS
If required for Windows AKS workloads configure gMSA
For finer control consider using a managed Kubelet Identity
If using AGIC, do not share an AppGW across clusters
Do not use AKS HTTP Routing Add-On, use instead the managed NGINX ingress with the application routing add-on.
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (isnull(properties.addonProfiles.httpApplicationRouting) or properties.addonProfiles.httpApplicationRouting.enabled==false) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: For Windows workloads use Accelerated Networking
Use the standard ALB (as opposed to the basic one)
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (tolower(properties.networkProfile.loadBalancerSku)=='standard') | distinct id,compliant

items.text: If using Azure CNI, consider using different Subnets for NodePools
Use Private Endpoints (preferred) or Virtual Network Service Endpoints to access PaaS services from the cluster
If hybrid connectivity is required, use 2xER or ER+VPN for better availability
Choose the best CNI network plugin for your requirements (Azure CNI recommended)
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (properties.networkProfile.networkPlugin=='azure') | distinct id,compliant

items.text: If using Azure CNI, size your subnet accordingly considering the maximum number of pods per node
If using Azure CNI, check the maximum pods/node (default 30)
If using private-IP LoadBalancer services, use a dedicated subnet (not the AKS subnet)
items.description: For internal apps organizations often open the whole AKS subnet in their firewalls. This opens network access to the nodes too, and potentially to the pods as well (if using Azure CNI). If LoadBalancer IPs are in a different subnet, only this one needs to be available to the app clients. Another reason is that if the IP addresses in the AKS subnet are a scarce resource, consuming its IP addresses for services will reduce the maximum scalability of the cluster.

items.text: Size the service IP address range accordingly (it is going to limit the cluster scalability)
If required add your own CNI plugin
If required configure Public IP per node in AKS
Use an ingress controller to expose web-based apps instead of exposing them with LoadBalancer-type services
Use Azure NAT Gateway as outboundType for scaling egress traffic
Use Dynamic allocations of IPs in order to avoid Azure CNI IP exhaustion
Filter egress traffic with AzFW/NVA if your security requirements mandate it
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (properties.networkProfile.outboundType=='userDefinedRouting') | distinct id,compliant

items.text: If using a public API endpoint, restrict the IP addresses that can access it
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = ((isnull(properties.apiServerAccessProfile.enablePrivateCluster) or properties.apiServerAccessProfile.enablePrivateCluster==false) and isnotnull(properties.apiServerAccessProfile.authorizedIPRanges)) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Use private clusters if your requirements mandate it
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | where isnotnull(properties.apiServerAccessProfile.enablePrivateCluster) | extend compliant = (properties.apiServerAccessProfile.enablePrivateCluster==true) | distinct id, compliant

items.text: For Windows 2019 and 2022 AKS nodes Calico Network Policies can be used
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | where isnotnull(properties.apiServerAccessProfile.enablePrivateCluster) | extend compliant = (properties.apiServerAccessProfile.enablePrivateCluster==true) | distinct id, compliant

items.text: Enable a Kubernetes Network Policy option (Calico/Azure)
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = isnotnull(properties.networkProfile.networkPolicy) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Use Kubernetes network policies to increase intra-cluster security
Use a WAF for web workloads (UIs or APIs)
Use DDoS Standard in the AKS Virtual Network
items.graph: Resources | where type=~'microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | project resourceGroup,name,pools=properties.agentPoolProfiles | mv-expand pools | project subnetId=tostring(pools.vnetSubnetID) | where isnotempty(subnetId) | join (Resources | where type=='microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | project id,resourceGroup,name,enableDdosProtection=tostring(properties.enableDdosProtection),subnets=properties.subnets | mv-expand subnets | project id,resourceGroup,name,enableDdosProtection,subnetId=tostring(subnets.id)) on subnetId | distinct id,resourceGroup,name,enableDdosProtection | extend compliant = (enableDdosProtection == 'true')

items.text: If required add company HTTP Proxy
items.graph: Resources | where type=~'microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | project resourceGroup,name,pools=properties.agentPoolProfiles | mv-expand pools | project subnetId=tostring(pools.vnetSubnetID) | where isnotempty(subnetId) | join (Resources | where type=='microsoft.network/virtualnetworks' | project id,resourceGroup,name,enableDdosProtection=tostring(properties.enableDdosProtection),subnets=properties.subnets | mv-expand subnets | project id,resourceGroup,name,enableDdosProtection,subnetId=tostring(subnets.id)) on subnetId | distinct id,resourceGroup,name,enableDdosProtection | extend compliant = (enableDdosProtection == 'true')

items.text: Consider using a service mesh for advanced microservice communication management
Configure alerts on the most critical metrics (see Container Insights for recommendations)
Check regularly Azure Advisor for recommendations on your cluster
Enable AKS auto-certificate rotation
Have a regular process to upgrade your kubernetes version periodically (quarterly, for example), or use the AKS autoupgrade feature
Use kured for Linux node upgrades in case you are not using node-image upgrade
Have a regular process to upgrade the cluster node images periodically (weekly, for example)
Consider gitops to deploy applications or cluster configuration to multiple clusters
Consider using AKS command invoke on private clusters
For planned events consider using Node Auto Drain
Develop own governance practices to make sure no changes are performed by operators in the node RG (aka 'infra RG')
Use custom Node RG (aka 'Infra RG') name
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (properties.nodeResourceGroup !startswith 'MC_') | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Do not use deprecated Kubernetes APIs in your YAML manifests
Taint Windows nodes
Keep windows containers patch level in sync with host patch level
Send master logs (aka API logs) to Azure Monitor or your preferred log management solution
items.description: Via Diagnostic Settings at the cluster level

items.text: If required use nodePool snapshots
Consider spot node pools for non time-sensitive workloads
Consider AKS virtual node for quick bursting
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (isnotnull(properties.addonProfiles.aciConnectorLinux) and properties.addonProfiles.aciConnectorLinux.enabled==true) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Monitor your cluster metrics with Container Insights (or other tools like Prometheus)
Store and analyze your cluster logs with Container Insights (or other tools like Telegraf/ElasticSearch)
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (isnotnull(properties.addonProfiles.omsagent) and properties.addonProfiles.omsagent.enabled==true) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Monitor CPU and memory utilization of the nodes
If using Azure CNI, monitor % of pod IPs consumed per node
Monitor OS disk queue depth in nodes
items.description: I/O in the OS disk is a critical resource. If the OS in the nodes gets throttled on I/O, this could lead to unpredictable behavior, typically ending up in node being declared NotReady

items.text: If not using egress filtering with AzFW/NVA, monitor standard ALB allocated SNAT ports
Subscribe to resource health notifications for your AKS cluster
Configure requests and limits in your pod specs
Enforce resource quotas for namespaces
Ensure your subscription has enough quota to scale out your nodepools
Configure Liveness and Readiness probes for all deployments
Use the Cluster Autoscaler
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (isnotnull(properties.autoScalerProfile)) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Customize node configuration for AKS node pools
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | extend compliant = (isnotnull(properties.austoscalerProfile)) | distinct id,compliant

items.text: Use the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler when required
Consider an appropriate node size, not too large or too small
items.description: Larger nodes will bring higher performance and features such as ephemeral disks and accelerated networking, but they will increase the blast radius and decrease the scaling granularity

items.text: If more than 5000 nodes are required for scalability then consider using an additional AKS cluster
Consider subscribing to EventGrid Events for AKS automation
For long running operation on an AKS cluster consider event termination
If required consider using Azure Dedicated Hosts for AKS nodes
Use ephemeral OS disks
items.graph: where type=='microsoft.containerservice/managedclusters' | project id,resourceGroup,name,pools=properties.agentPoolProfiles | mvexpand pools | extend compliant = (pools.osDiskType=='Ephemeral') | project id,name=strcat(name,'-',pools.name), resourceGroup, compliant

items.text: For non-ephemeral disks, use high IOPS and larger OS disks for the nodes when running many pods/node since it requires high performance for running multiple pods and will generate huge logs with default AKS log rotation thresholds
For hyper performance storage option use Ultra Disks on AKS
Avoid keeping state in the cluster, and store data outside (AzStorage, AzSQL, Cosmos, etc)
If using AzFiles Standard, consider AzFiles Premium and/or ANF for performance reasons

If using Azure Disks and AZs, consider having nodepools within a zone for LRS disk with VolumeBindingMode:WaitForFirstConsumer for provisioning storage in right zone or use ZRS disk for nodepools spanning multiple zones
